class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdliytk-l-tr/" target="_blank" >Walking the paths of Nat King Cole and Louis Armstrong in Bronzeville</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdliytk-l-ty/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p>On Saturday afternoon, Myron Stokes stood in a Bronzeville performance venue that's nearly 130 years old.</p><p>The room was dark, the walls were worn and the stage was vacant. But he said he could envision what the space was like in the 1920s, '30s and '40s.</p><p>"I hear the music, the dancing and the snapping of fingers," said Stokes, 56, of Oak Park, who also imagined what life may have been like for Black crowds back then.</p><p>Stokes was part of a group that explored the historic Forum Hall at 318-328 E. 43rd St., where jazz artists including Nat King Cole and Sun Ra played. The tour closed out a day of arts and activities, including performances in the soon-to-open Forum Cafe next door and a neighborhood walk to the homes and haunts of jazz icons.</p><p>The free event was part of Chicago’s monthlong celebration of UNESCO International Jazz Day, which culminates in an all-star global concert Thursday at Lyric Opera House.</p><p>Event organizers said they hoped attendees felt more connected to their communities and the people within.</p><p>"We want neighbors to meet each other," said Shani "Lady Sunshine" Smith of Calumet Heights, who led the outdoor tour. "We want people to know the histories of their communities and connect around that and take ownership of their communities."</p><p><a class="Link" entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Review: Maya Hawke shows another side of her character in intimate show at Thalia Hall
entries.summary: <p>The “Stranger Things” effect was in full force Sunday night as Maya Hawke, aka Robin Buckley, settled into Thalia Hall to showcase her other character: singer-songwriter. It wasn’t just because the all-ages show was populated by a number of tweens and their parents who screamed messages of admiration and clamored to get tour merch and bags of popcorn from the bar, but the show itself was a bit, well, upside down because she largely performed material that no one has even heard yet..</p><p>Rather than a traditional concert with full band, lights, props and all the other bells and whistles, “The Evening With Maya Hawke” featured just her and producer/new husband Christian Lee Hutson on acoustic guitars and violinist Odessa Jorgensen. They were all seated on chairs, carefully unpacking the material from Hawke’s upcoming folk-pop album “Maitreya Corso” over the course of an hour and some change.</p><p>The record isn’t out until May 1 and only two singles have been released thus far: the plucky “Devil You Know” and whimsical ballad “Bring Home My Man,” which made the untraditional format of Hawke’s latest tour (her first in three years) feel more like an intimate listening party. Or, possibly an extension of the in-store meet and greets she’s been queuing up, including Wicker Park’s Reckless Records on Saturday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0d0000" name="image-0d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2ac38e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2Fd0%2F3e1fec27488cb35bc0792189b588%2Fmt3-2634-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d913257/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2Fd0%2F3e1fec27488cb35bc0792189b588%2Fmt3-2634-1.jpg" alt="Maya Hawke performs with violinist Odessa Jorgensen and producer/new husband Christian Lee Hutson on acoustic guitar Sunday at Thalia Hall." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d913257/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2Fd0%2F3e1fec27488cb35bc0792189b588%2Fmt3-2634-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e91b24d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2Fd0%2F3e1fec27488cb35bc0792189b588%2Fmt3-2634-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d913257/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F50%2Fd0%2F3e1fec27488cb35bc0792189b588%2Fmt3-2634-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Maya Hawke performs with violinist Odessa Jorgensen and producer/new husband Christian Lee Hutson on acoustic guitar Sunday at Thalia Hall.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Melissa Tamez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I know this is unusual and different,” Hawke admitted to the audience at the sold-out venue, “but I wanted to take you on a journey through this record.” A journey, she clarified, that was about evolving from a “painful to a positive place,” which was felt in confessional lyrics about love lost and gained and the growing pains of growing up.</p><p>In promotional materials, “Maitreya Corso” is described as an alter ego for Hawke, a “magical misfit” who lives in a “fantasy world” with an “inability to adapt to the surrounding world [that] allows her to create a world of her own.” While the night felt like a missed opportunity to not bring in some of those fantastical elements (perhaps it’s being saved for an upcoming tour), the choice for an intimate, up-close introduction to the material served its purpose, too — especially for allowing Hawke’s eccentric personality to shine.</p><p>Equal time was spent on the conversational banter between songs, and as Hawke quipped about everything from Malort to Miley Cyrus and sex toys (eventually apologizing to the parents in the room for her frequent cursing and talk of vibrators), it gave a window into how the wheels in her brain turn that allows for such unique and densely worded material.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5b0000" name="image-5b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f0a8b96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f04499/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf0a5f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Maya Hawke speaks to the audience during a concert at Thalia Hall in Chicago on Sunday, April 26, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf0a5f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/565ad17/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf0a5f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4916x2759+0+263/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2F83%2Ff1520bba4bd7a44f24bf39f7a189%2Fmt1-1462.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Maya Hawke speaks to the audience Sunday during her concert at Thalia Hall.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Melissa Tamez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Fast-lipped songs like “Great Minds” and “Last Thoughts on Morning Star” had the pace of a poetry slam open mic, padded with Hawke’s even-tempered vocals that would’ve fit well in the coffee shop, too. She was helped by Jorgenson and Hutson on lush three-part harmonies that were reminiscent of the ’70s folk scene in New York, not far from where Hawke grew up with her actor parents Uma Thurman and Ethan Hawke.</p><p>On Sunday, though, she name-dropped other famous figures important to her artistic journey, like citing Leonard Cohen as personal inspiration and instructing the room to go home and devour his work, “How To Speak Poetry.” She also covered a few lines of Paul Simon’s “Still Crazy After All These Years,” emphasizing the throughline between that song and her own, “Terms of Estrangement.”</p><p>The track, she said, was written as a “breakup song on delay” after she and Hutson ran into one of her exes on the street and were inspired by the exchange rather than feel cut down by it. The couple’s natural chemistry oozed from the stage. On the tender “Love Of My Life,” Hawke appeared to sing the lyrics right to him, and on the gripping “Heavy Rain,” his passionate guitar playing coaxed Hawke into giving one of the most soulful performances of the night.</p><p>As Hawke explained, she and Hutson (also a recording artist, formerly of The Driftwood Singers) fell in love while making the record, and it’s their first time touring together. There’s a sense he’s helping push her out of her comfort zone, too — this tour is Hawke’s first time joining Hutson on guitar, which she described as “conquering a fear.”</p><p>It’s all setting Hawke up for a lucrative music career with “Stranger Things” now in the can. While the Netflix phenomenon may have drawn an obvious fan crowd to Hawke’s show on Sunday, it also had a powerful way of pushing music stars (Kate Bush) into the public consciousness. Hawke will rightfully be next in line.</p><h3>Set list for Maya Hawke April 26, 2026 show at Thalia Hall</h3><p>Devil You Know<br>Love of My Life<br>Great Minds<br>Terms of Estrangement<br>Dream House<br>Slacker in the Rye<br>Last Thoughts on Morning Star<br>Last Living Lost Cause<br>Heavy Rain<br>Lioness<br>Bring Home My Man<br>Luna Moth<br>Thérèse</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz. Many were accused of assaulting or resisting federal agents or officers.</p><p>Two defendants have pleaded guilty. Twenty have been cleared. Grand jurors refused to indict at least three of the 20, and another member of that group was found not guilty at trial.</p><p>Meanwhile, prosecutors have entered into deferred-prosecution agreements with four additional defendants. In each of those cases, if that person abides by certain terms over a set period of time, the feds have agreed to drop the charges in that case as well.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times is tracking all of the known criminal cases tied to Operation Midway Blitz:</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6e0000" entries.title: Sky minority investors rally around principal owner Michael Alter
entries.summary: <p>Twenty minority investors in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> have declared they do not support <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/01/sky-principal-owner-michael-alter-sued-by-early-minority-investor-over-ownership-stake" >a lawsuit brought against principal owner Michael Alter</a> by fellow investor Steven Rogers, according to court documents.</p><p>The declarations were included in a motion to dismiss the case filed by Alter’s counsel, Robert Chapman. Much of that motion remains redacted, including the names of the minority investors. </p><p>On Monday, Chapman requested approval to file the unredacted version under seal, citing a confidentiality clause in the Sky’s operating agreement that protects sensitive financial provisions and identities.</p><p>While Rogers’ counsel previously used similar reasoning to file their initial complaint under seal, they objected to this request. They argued the legal analysis for sealing documents changes once that information becomes the basis for a judicial decision, such as a motion to dismiss. They must submit their argument in writing by May 6.</p><p>Rogers filed the initial complaint in February, alleging unlawful self-dealing from Alter that unfairly diluted minority interests. The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/18/court-seals-complaint-against-sky-owner-michael-alter-debt-conversion-at-center-of-lawsuit" >complaint centers on a 2023 capital raise</a> where the Sky brought in $8.5 million from a small group of investors. Around that time, Alter converted his loans into equity. The core of the dispute is whether that debt-for-equity swap was conducted properly and authorized by the operating agreement.</p><p>Alter’s motion to dismiss argues the transaction was expressly permitted and that the conversion did not occur on preferential terms.</p><p>“What Rogers mistakenly characterizes as wrongful self-dealing was an expressly permitted transaction that inured to the benefit of all shareholders including Rogers,” the motion states.</p><p>The motion also states Rogers is an inadequate representative for a derivative suit because of the documented opposition from other investors.</p><p>“The Team’s other investors—the very same people whose interests Rogers purports to protect in this derivative suit—oppose this lawsuit and Rogers’ harmful and baseless attacks on the Team and Michael Alter,” the motion states. “These declarants not only support Michael Alter’s leadership, but also emphasize that Rogers’ lawsuit risks harming the Sky’s reputation and these members’ investment, and has already attracted negative nationwide press coverage about the Sky, as Rogers intended.”</p><p>One minority investor, Linda Friedman, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/18/court-seals-complaint-against-sky-owner-michael-alter-debt-conversion-at-center-of-lawsuit" >spoke to the Sun-Times in February</a> in defense of the debt-to-equity swap. Friedman, who believes she is the team's third-largest investor, said there was complete disclosure and that the valuation used for Alter’s loan conversion was fair. She believes that the capital raise would not have been possible without a transaction to improve the balance sheet.</p><p>However, another investor who spoke on the condition of anonymity said he did not realize Alter’s loans were convertible and did not understand how the conversion was valued.</p><p>Alter’s motion to dismiss also argues that the complaint improperly names Alter as an individual defendant rather than the LLC manager. It further claims the lawsuit impermissibly alleges both breach of contract and breach of fiduciary duty, calling the claims duplicative.</p><p>The defense will have until May 21 to reply once Rogers' response to the motion is filed.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Federal charges filed over gun used in fatal shooting of Chicago officer
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors on Monday charged a woman who allegedly lied in 2024 while purchasing the 10mm Glock pistol that would eventually be used to kill<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/27/suspect-charged-in-swedish-hospital-shooting-that-left-1-cop-dead-and-another-fighting-for-his-life-source" target="_blank" >Chicago police officer John<b> </b>Bartholomew</a> and wound a second officer.</p><p>Olivia<b> </b>Burgos faces one count of making a false statement while purchasing a firearm, as outlined in a criminal complaint filed in federal court in Hammond, Indiana. Her attorney could not immediately be reached for comment.</p><p>Bartholomew, 38, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/25/officers-reportedly-injured-shooting-chicago-swedish-hospital-lincoln-square" target="_blank" >shot alongside the second officer</a> who was gravely wounded Saturday at Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital. Alphanso Talley, 26, has been charged in the shooting. </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/27/suspect-charged-in-swedish-hospital-shooting-that-left-1-cop-dead-and-another-fighting-for-his-life-source" >Man shot Chicago cops, killing one, after undressing and pulling gun from beneath blanket at Swedish Hospital</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The feds say Burgos purchased the gun used in the incident on May 27, 2024, from Range USA in Merrillville, Indiana. She claimed on a form to be the actual purchaser and denied being addicted to illegal drugs at the time, according to the complaint. </p><p>She also allegedly claimed to be living in La Porte, Indiana, at the time.</p><p>But when interviewed by law enforcement on Saturday, Burgos allegedly said she'd been addicted to fentanyl since April 23, 2024, and had used the drug daily ever since. She also acknowledged using fentanyl<b> </b>the day she bought the gun, the feds say. </p><p>In her interview with the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, Burgos allegedly said she bought the gun<b> </b>for her boyfriend at the time. She said she believed him to be a convicted felon, and she said he gave her money for the gun, according to the complaint. </p><p>Finally, Burgos allegedly told the ATF that she actually lived in Highland, Indiana, at the time of the purchase.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/21/71/c8c67a8c427e8b8057229cf2f9ac/burgoscomplaint.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Vendedor ambulante de La Villita asesinado en tiroteo es recordado como un 'hombre de familia'
entries.summary: <p>Un vendedor ambulante del vecindario de La Villita estaba trabajando a solo poca distancia de su esposa e hijo el miércoles de la semana pasada cuando fue asesinado en un tiroteo que también hirió a tres mujeres.</p><p>Antonio “Coco” Padilla, de 46 años, es recordado como un querido vendedor en La Villita, un “padre devoto de [cinco hijos], hermano y un amigo apreciado por muchos”, escribió su familia en un <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-for-antonio-padillas-family?cdn-cache=0" target="_blank" >GoFundMe</a> creado para recaudar fondos para los gastos del funeral.</p><p>El tiroteo ocurrió a eso de las 4:30 p.m. cerca de la esquina de la calle 26th y la St. Louis Avenue, según la Policía de Chicago. La policía no divulgó más detalles ni proporcionó un motivo.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b10000" name="module-b10000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Estella Aguilera trabaja en una tienda de novias cercana con su madre y estaba ayudando a un cliente en el mostrador el miércoles cuando escucharon “muchos ruidos de explosiones”, dijo.</p><p>“Miré a mi mamá y le dije, ‘Esos no eran cohetes.’ Y entonces ella dijo, ‘Cierra la puerta, cierra la puerta’”, comentó Aguilera.</p><p>Un par de minutos después, Aguilera salió y “vio a personas en el suelo”.</p><p>Fue entonces cuando escuchó a la esposa de Padilla gritar su nombre y se dio cuenta de que durante el tiroteo, ella y su hijo de un año habían estado en la camioneta de Padilla, estacionada junto a su carrito de frutas en la calle 26.</p><p>Padilla fue baleado varias veces y fue declarado muerto en el Hospital Mount Sinai, dijeron las autoridades.</p><p>Las tres mujeres que fueron baleadas fueron llevadas a Mount Sinai y sobrevivieron. Dos de ellas también eran vendedoras ambulantes; la otra era una clienta, añadió Aguilera. Una tenía 25 años, otra 42 y la tercera 47.</p><p>Aguilera se encontraba regularmente con Padilla y su familia por el vecindario. Era un “muchacho agradable” y un “hombre de familia”, dijo.</p><p>“A las 2 p.m. [el miércoles], estaba allí y le compré fruta”, relató Aguilera. “Él solo estaba hablando de lo grande que está su bebé y que ahora ya camina. Le dije, ‘Es tu gemelo, se parece a ti'. Y él respondió, ‘Sí, míralo’”.</p><p>“Para mí, él era un hombre de familia," dijo Aguilera. "Siempre tenía a sus hijos con él y a su esposa a su lado".</p><p>La Policía de Chicago divulgó el jueves imágenes de vigilancia que muestran a tres personas buscadas en relación con el tiroteo.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6567e8" name="image-6567e8"></a> <picture hombre muerto y tres mujeres heridas el miércoles pasado en La Villita.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Departamento de Policía de Chicago</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los videos muestran a tres personas en un sedán blanco en una gasolinera poco antes del tiroteo. Las tres personas se ven usando cubrebocas, sudaderas oscuras y pantalones oscuros. La policía las describió como personas que pesan entre 160 y 180 libras y miden entre 5 pies 10 pulgadas y 6 pies.</p><p>Los Crime Stoppers del Condado de Cook ofrecen una recompensa de $10,000 a cualquier persona que proporcione información que conduzca a un arresto por el tiroteo.</p><p>Se pide a cualquier persona con información que se comunique con los detectives del Área 4 al 312-746-8252. Se puede enviar información anónima a <a class="Link" href="http://cpdtip.com" target="_blank" >cpdtip.com</a>.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Burlington Central grad Drew Scharnowski transfers to Duke
entries.summary: <p>From Burlington to a Blue Blood.</p><p>When Drew Sharnowski, the former Burlington Central star, hit the transfer portal this spring following his redshirt sophomore season at Belmont, he instantly became a recruiting priority for high-major programs. And why not?</p><p>Scharnowski is a blossoming big man with a high level of athleticism and fluidity to go with his 6-9 size. He runs the floor, soars above the rim with high-flying dunks and blocks shots. Throw in the fact he plays the biggest position of need in college basketball and Scharnowski was immediately coveted.</p><p>There was an endless list of high-major suitors and options, including much of the Big Ten. He could have also stayed right in Nashville, where Belmont is located, and played in the SEC for Vanderbilt, a program that was in pursuit right to the end.</p><p>But along came Duke, the Blue Blood program. Coach Jon Scheyer and his staff became enamored with an emerging player who fit what they were looking for.</p><p>Scharnowski, who put up 10.7 points, six rebounds and 2.6 assists a game and was a first-team All-Missouri Valley Conference player, recently committed to Duke.</p><p>“Growing up, playing at Duke was always a dream, so in that sense I definitely imagined it,” Scharnowski said. “But having them actually reach out during the portal process was unexpected and really special.”</p><p>Like everyone involved in the transfer market, Scharnowski says he felt the speed of the portal process. But he also knew he needed to put a whole lot of thought in his decision process. Those two don’t always work simultaneously together.</p><p>“I didn’t want to get caught up in everything that comes with a name like Duke,” Scharnowski admitted. “I tried to stay grounded throughout the process and lean on my support system.”</p><p>But in the end it was impossible to ignore all that Duke had to offer. He also gained self-assurance in knowing how Duke approaches the portal.</p><p>“Duke’s reputation speaks for itself,” Scharnowski said. “They develop winners and NBA players. They’re also very selective in the portal, so I knew that if they were recruiting me, they really believed in me and saw how I could fit into their system. That gave me a lot of confidence that it was the right place.”</p><p>Always the prototype “high-upside prospect,” Schnarnowski tapped into that this past season after redshirting as a freshman and playing a limited role off the bench a year ago. Even as a college player, Schnarnowski is still just scratching the surface of what he can become.</p><p>But over the past year, Scharnowski made it clear to see where he’s headed as a player. He turned the corner this past season, becoming more aggressive and using his athleticism to at times overwhelm opponents at the mid-major level.</p><p>Burlington Central coach Brett Porto saw the potential first-hand and then has watched his former star blossom in college. Porto is quick to point out that it always went beyond the easy-to-see talent when it came to Scharnowski.</p><p>“When you match how hard he played, and how he wanted the best for his teammates, with his work ethic, I think his confidence in his abilities just continues to grow and take him to new heights,” Porto said.</p><p>“It’s a mix of maturity and physical development,” Schnarnowski said of his steady rise. “I had to grow into my body, and a lot of credit goes to our strength coach at Belmont, coach Lee Scott. He really invested in me, both as a player and a person.”</p><p>With the added production and the improved physical development, the confidence began to soar for Scharnowski. Plus, digging into what made him originally fall in love with the game helped his overall mindset.</p><p>“The biggest thing has been keeping that same free-flowing style I had when I was younger,” Scharnowski said. “Growing up I played with a pure love and joy for the game, and holding onto that joy has been a big part of my success. My faith has also played a big role in that. It keeps me grounded and reminds me that I am someone who plays basketball, but basketball is not what defines me.”</p><p>He believes that approach has taken the pressure off of him and allows him to play more freely. It’s also the impetus of his belief going forward, whether it’s playing in the Missouri Valley Conference or the premier college basketball program in the country.</p><p>“I try to carry a level of confidence where I truly believe I can achieve anything through my work and preparation,” Scharnowski said. “My long-term goal hasn’t changed — it’s always been the NBA — and I believe Duke gives me the best opportunity and resources to reach that. I also want to be a part of continuing Duke’s tradition and competing for a national championship.”<br></p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Matt DeCaro, longtime Chicago actor, dies at 70
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p>Chicago actor Matt DeCaro never took being a working actor for granted.</p><p>Last week, before an unusually early-in-the-day staging of August Wilson’s “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom” at the Goodman Theatre, artistic director Susan Booth bumped into DeCaro as she entered the Goodman through the loading dock. Lamenting their cockcrow schedule, she asked Mr. DeCaro how he was holding up.</p><p>“Matt had a grin on his face and he looked quizzically at me, like ‘What an odd question?’ And he said, ‘I’m doing great. We get to do this,’” Booth recalled, noting that it was the last conversation she had with Mr. DeCaro. “And I've been thinking a lot about that: We get to do this.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Matt DeCaro (center) played the role of Sturdyvant in The Goodman’s production of “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom” with From left, Jabari Khaliq, Al’Jaleel McGhee, Cedric Young and Kelvin Roston Jr.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mr. DeCaro performed in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/08/review-ma-raineys-black-bottom-goodman-theatre" >production of the play</a> Friday night and died early Saturday at his Bridgeport home from heart failure, his wife, Sheila O’Callaghan, said. He was 70.</p><p>After receiving word of his death, the Saturday matinee production of “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom” was canceled, but the cast decided collectively to carry on with Saturday evening’s performance with the help of Mr. DeCaro’s understudy, Scott Aiello.</p><p>“They expressed a pretty strong desire to do it because they felt that’s what he’d want them to do,” Booth said. “It was brave and highly emotional. They were extraordinary.”</p><p>Mr. DeCaro played Sturdyvant, the owner of a Chicago recording studio in the 1920s who had a contentious relationship with Ma Rainey, known as “Mother of the Blues.”</p><p>Booth said sparks flew on stage between Mr. DeCaro and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/23/chicago-blues-ma-raineys-black-bottom-goodman-theatre-e-faye-butler" >E. Faye Butler,</a> who stars as Ma Rainey.</p><p>“You look at them nose to nose on stage and each at the absolute top of their game,” said Booth. “She recently told me that every time she was on stage with Matt, she had to be her best, and it gave her permission to be her best.”</p><p>Mr. DeCaro was a beloved figure in the acting community.</p><p>“The hallmark of Matt, on and off stage, was he always made whoever he was talking to feel like they were much more interesting than he was. His generosity and curiosity made the best scene partner, but also a lovely friend off stage because he was just always intrigued by whoever he was talking to, and you realize how rare that is,” Booth said.</p><p>“Anybody who worked with him wanted to work with him again,” Booth said.</p><p>“With actors, there’s a couple ways you audition. One is a legit audition. But another is who you are in the process, and, if you are a delight to be in the room with, people want you back,” Booth said. “And the guy could do anything from musicals to straight plays to Shakespeare and Chekhov, but he was also just dreamy in the room. You wanted to be in the room with him.”</p><p>Mr. DeCaro’s stage career spanned four decades across major stages in Chicago and around the country.</p><p>At a Goodman festival in 2006 honoring playwright David Mamet, actor John Mahoney had to back out of a leading role in the theater’s production of “Romance,” and Mr. DeCaro was tapped to play his part.</p><p>“Matt could not have been more gracious, affable and charismatic, and he really embodies the spirit of the Chicago theater community,” said Denise Schneider, director of communications at the Goodman. “There have been so many beautiful tributes on social media to him.”</p><p>Mr. DeCaro also had various film and television roles, including on “Prison Break,” “Chicago P.D.” and “Curb Your Enthusiasm,” in which he played a doctor who was tapped by the show’s star Larry David to investigate whether a bingo game at the senior home where his father lived was rigged.</p><p>Mr. DeCaro was born April 2, 1956, to Matthew DeCaro Sr. and Laura Fitzpatrick DeCaro, who both grew up in Canaryville. He was a banker. She worked in the audio visual department for the University of Illinois.</p><p>Mr. DeCaro was raised near Marquette Park and went to St. Laurence High School, where he acted in his first play “Fiddler on the Roof.”</p><p>He worked for many years as a special education teacher at The Chicago Academy for the Arts, picking up acting gigs when he was able to, his wife said.</p><p>“He went to college and got a teaching degree at University of Illinois Chicago but always wanted to act,” she said. “He loved acting. He was a craftsman, so meticulous about the work.”</p><p>Mr. DeCaro especially treasured working on productions affiliated with a university that included student actors.</p><p>“He just loved teaching and guiding the students and making sure they were learning the right way,” his wife said.</p><p>“Many young artists looked to him as a mentor,” said Jim McCaffrey, DeCaro’s agent at Stewart Talent.</p><p>In addition to his wife, Mr. DeCaro is survived by his son, Fitzpatrick DeCaro, as well as three grandchildren.</p><p>Services are pending.</p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Cubs pitcher Ryan Rolison followed a long and winding road to the big leagues
entries.summary: <p>SAN DIEGO — Fifty days in the life of a professional baseball player. Fasten your seat belts, and then remind yourself afterward of how glamorous that life is.</p><p><b>Nov. 18, 2025:</b> Pitcher Ryan Rolison, a former first-round draft pick of the Colorado Rockies who had one big-league win to his name after eight seasons in pro ball, was taken off the team’s 40-man roster. The move was not unexpected. Rolison said the Rockies had called a couple of days earlier to inform him.</p><p><b>Nov. 19, 2025:</b> “Not 12 hours later,’’ Rolison said, “the Rockies called me back and said the Braves have traded for me.’’ Officially, it went in the books as a cash transaction.</p><p>“Being from Tennessee,’’ he said, “a lot of people, my family and close friends, are Braves fans. Everybody’s fired up.’’</p><p><b>Dec. 12, 2025:</b> “Two weeks go by, and the Braves called and said, ‘We need to make a move. So I was on waivers again, and got picked up the next day by the White Sox.”</p><p><b>Dec. 23, 2025:</b> “I was on the roster a couple of weeks, and the White Sox call me on December 23rd and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/12/23/white-sox-sign-sean-newcomb-designate-ryan-rolison-for-assignment" >take me off the roster.”</a> Merry Christmas, Ryan.</p><p><b>Dec. 24, 2025-Jan. 6, 2026:</b> Ryan is a man without a team. The new year begins in limbo.</p><p><b>Jan. 7, 2026:</b> Ryan and his fiancee, Lauren Hoselton, are on a plane to Chicago. They are to be married three days later in the University Club. Before the plane takes off, Ryan is awaiting a call to find out whether he had been claimed on waivers by another club. On the plane, of course, no cell service.</p><p>When the couple arrives in Chicago, news. “I get claimed by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs.</a> It was a cool way to start off the weekend. My wife’s from Bloomington (Illinois) and Chicago’s our favorite city.’’</p><p>A welcome wedding present, made all the sweeter in the span of 11 days in April.</p><p><b>April 14:</b> Rolison is promoted from Triple-A Iowa to the big leagues after Ethan Roberts cuts a finger on his pitching hand. He joins the Cubs in Philadelphia, then pitches a mop-up inning in a 10-4 win over the Phillies. He then sits and waits for 11 days.</p><p><b>April 24:</b> Cubs manager Craig Counsell has a tired bullpen. Caleb Thielbar goes on the 15-day injured list with a hamstring strain. The Cubs fall behind the Dodgers, 4-0, after five. Counsell summons Rolison to pitch the sixth. Then the seventh. Then the eighth.</p><p>The Cubs rally. Alex Bregman hits a game-tying home run in the eighth. Dansby Swanson hits a two-run home run in the ninth to win it 6-4. Winning pitcher: Ryan Rolison, his second career win in the big leagues.</p><p>“Ryan Rolison, to me, is the story of the game,’’ a grateful Counsell says. “He hasn’t pitched in [11 days] and gives an effort like that, three innings. I’m not sure he ever pitched three innings in a bigger game, and goes through the Dodger lineup,</p><p>“That was really impressive.’’</p><h3>This moment was a long time in coming</h3><p>Ryan was shagging in the outfield one day in 2021 while with the Rockies and a line drive he did not see struck him in his pitching hand, fracturing the middle finger of his pitching hand. He was out for three months. He has had two shoulder surgeries. The rehab lasted 2½ years. He has had an appendectomy.</p><p>Then you have a Friday night, in front of 53,000 people in Dodger Stadium.</p><p>“You can easily get frustrated when you look at the guys you were drafted with and see their paths to the big leagues. For a long time, I think that just ate me up, being injured. But I finally came to terms with, everyone’s got their own path.</p><p>“So it’s like trusting and staying patient. There’s a lot of times I could have laid down and quit ... but I kept going. I kept sticking with the process, and it’s paid off.</p><p>“It’s special. I love it here.’’</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" entries.title: Federal appeals court upholds conviction of ex-Illinois House Speaker Michael Madigan
entries.summary: <p>The federal appeals court in Chicago on Monday upheld the historic conviction of former Illinois House Speaker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news" target="_blank" >Michael J. Madigan</a>, finding no error<b> </b>in the four-month trial that ultimately led to his lengthy prison sentence.</p><p>"Michael Madigan spent nearly a decade leveraging his power as one of the highest-ranking public officials in Illinois in exchange for over $3 million of financial benefits for his close political allies," a three-judge appeals panel concluded. "… Madigan insists that this was run-of-the-mill politics. But a jury of twelve Illinois residents saw the evidence differently. So do we."</p><p>The 29-page opinion from the 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals comes less than three weeks after<b> </b>oral arguments and will likely prompt a bid by Madigan to the U.S. Supreme Court. Judge Michael Scudder, an appointee of President Donald Trump's, wrote the opinion.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news/2026/04/09/madigan-corruption-conviction-appeal-federal-7th-circuit" >Madigan’s landmark conviction now in hands of appeals court focused on ‘specific’ corruption</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Joining Scudder on the panel was Judge Frank Easterbrook, appointed by President Ronald Reagan; and Judge Nancy Maldonado, appointed by President Joe Biden. </p><p>A federal jury in February 2025 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news/mike-madigan-guilty-verdict" target="_blank" >convicted Madigan</a> of a bribery conspiracy, wire fraud and other crimes. U.S. District Judge John Blakey handed Madigan a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/06/13/mike-madigan-sentencing-hearing" target="_blank" >7 ½-year prison sentence</a> four months later, finding that Madigan lied to the jury.</p><p>Madigan <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news/2025/10/13/former-illinois-house-speaker-michael-madigan-is-in-prison" target="_blank" >then surrendered</a> to a minimum-security prison camp in West Virginia the following October, capping a massive federal corruption probe that dated all the way back to 2014.</p><p>Madigan’s conviction centered on two schemes. In one, ComEd paid five Madigan allies $1.3 million over eight years so Madigan would look more favorably at the utility’s legislation. The money was funneled through third-party firms, and the recipients did hardly any work for ComEd.</p><p>The other involved a deal to have then-Chicago Ald. Danny Solis installed on a state board in exchange for Solis’ help landing private business for Madigan’s tax appeal law firm.</p><p>Madigan appealed, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news/2026/04/09/madigan-corruption-conviction-appeal-federal-7th-circuit" target="_blank" >his attorney pointed</a> to at least three problems with his conviction. Amy Saharia argued the ComEd allegations were “far too vague,”<b> </b>that there were errors in the jury instructions, and that prosecutors failed to prove a quid pro quo between Madigan and Solis.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/06/13/mike-madigan-sentencing-hearing" >Michael Madigan sentenced to 7.5 years in prison for corruption convictions</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The former speaker has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-corruption-trials/2025/08/01/madigan-heading-to-appeals-court-with-lawyers-who-convinced-supreme-court-to-narrow-bribery-law" target="_blank" >also enlisted Lisa Blatt</a>, a well-known high court litigator who claims an 82% win rate before the U.S. Supreme Court. That suggests Madigan is already looking past Chicago’s federal appeals court.</p><p>Madigan led the Illinois House of Representatives for all but two years between 1983 and 2021. He held onto the gavel for two years after the feds’ investigation went public in 2019.</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Chicago arts leaders back proposed law aimed at fake tickets
entries.summary: <p>Leaders of Ravinia, Joffrey Ballet, Lyric Opera and other prominent Chicago arts groups joined together Monday to voice support for state legislation that would bar deceptive ticketing practices that they warn are on the rise.</p><p>Speculative ticketing, or ghost ticketing, is when a third-party seller lists tickets that they do not have. Audience members may think they have bought a legitimate ticket, only to arrive at the show with a fake. The problem has been rampant in the stadium concert industry, but the group Monday underscored that the fraudulent ticket problem now is impacting their local venues, not just national headliners like Taylor Swift.</p><p>Leaders say the practice threatens both their reputations and bottom lines.</p><p>At Downtown’s Lyric Opera House, the venue leaders were joined by two Illinois State lawmakers — Rep. Nabeela Syed, D-Palatine, and Sen. Steve Stadelman, D-Rockford — who are supporting the legislation to ban the practice.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-570000" name="image-570000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d283cc7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6143x3448+0+325/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F81%2F6a0369b844c4ad0ce4a4876d965c%2Fticketscamleg-2604280326.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a80e86e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6143x3448+0+325/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F81%2F6a0369b844c4ad0ce4a4876d965c%2Fticketscamleg-2604280326.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="TICKETSCAMLEG_2604280326.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a80e86e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6143x3448+0+325/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F81%2F6a0369b844c4ad0ce4a4876d965c%2Fticketscamleg-2604280326.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81492b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6143x3448+0+325/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F81%2F6a0369b844c4ad0ce4a4876d965c%2Fticketscamleg-2604280326.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a80e86e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6143x3448+0+325/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F81%2F6a0369b844c4ad0ce4a4876d965c%2Fticketscamleg-2604280326.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Arts leaders, including Jeffrey P. Haydon of Ravinia, say speculative ticketing, or ghost ticketing, threatens both their reputations and bottom lines.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Every day, patrons are being sold what they believe are valid tickets, when, in reality, they are only paying for a chance that someone may be able to secure a seat,” said John Mangum, Lyric’s general director, who was also joined by leaders of The Auditorium and Harris Theater.</p><p>“This practice leads to confusion, frustration, and what many in our field now call ‘front-gate heartbreak,’ when audiences arrive at a performance only to learn that they do not actually have a valid admission ticket.”</p><p>For venue operators, the concern goes beyond fielding disappointment from individual customers. It threatens their bottom line at a time when many are already hurting. A <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2026/02/03/chicago-indie-venues-health-metro-ramova-salt-shed" target="_blank" ><u>report released earlier this year</u></a> found that nearly three out of four independent live entertainment venues in the city are currently not profitable, as they reel from rising artist fees, higher taxes and soaring labor and production costs.</p><p>“These businesses are already navigating rising costs and thin margins and speculative ticketing only makes it harder for them to survive by undermining the integrity of their ticket sales and eroding trust with fans,” Syed said.</p><p>The legislation passed the Illinois House unanimously on April 15 and now awaits action in the Senate. It would ban resellers from posting tickets they do not possess at the time of the listing. It would also strengthen consumer recourse by adding the ability to file complaints and prompt investigations by the attorney general’s office.</p><p>Several states, including <a class="Link" href="https://www.mprnews.org/story/2025/01/01/new-minnesota-laws-2025-january-1" target="_blank" ><u>Minnesota</u></a>, have passed similar legislation, and a federal ban on speculative ticketing was <a class="Link" href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/281/text" target="_blank" ><u>introduced last year</u></a>, although it stalled in Congress.</p><p>Mangum said he’s seen an uptick in speculative ticketing following the COVID-19 pandemic. Lyric has seen it happen most during runs of popular shows, like Billy Corgan’s “<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2025/11/18/billy-corgan-smashing-pumpkins-night-of-mellon-collie-infinite-sadness-lyric-opera" target="_blank" ><u>Night of Mellon Collie and Infinite Sadness</u></a>” or during the Joffrey’s “Nutcracker.” But, Mangum said it also emerges during more traditional operatic performances, including the company’s recent production of “Madama Butterfly.”</p><p>In addition to listings on third-party sites like StubHub, speculative tickets are often found on believable look-alike sites with convincing names that use real company logos and artist photos. They often advertise that tickets are nearly sold out — creating artificial scarcity and inflating prices for shows, sometimes before real tickets have even gone on sale.</p><p>“So the consumer thinks they're being savvy and buying from the right place, and they show up at the door, and they don't have a ticket,” Mangum said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0c0000" name="image-0c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/21849ba/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f8fd38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7ce9150/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="TICKETSCAMLEG_2604280049.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7ce9150/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5554a51/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7ce9150/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7997x4488+0+423/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb7%2F0a%2F181d34a74a8f9f270a8000750fe1%2Fticketscamleg-2604280049.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rep. Nabeela Syed, D-Palatine, backed proposed legislation in the Illinois Statehouse that would ban resellers from posting tickets they do not possess at the time of the listing.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>To address the issue, Lyric’s box office sets aside tickets to ensure patrons who arrive with fake tickets can still see the performance. But that means lost ticket revenue, measuring anywhere from $2,500 to $5,000 per night, Mangum estimates. Over the course of the season, that adds up.</p><p>“This is not a small or isolated issue,” Mangum said. “It is a structural problem that affects audiences, artists and the broader cultural economy.”</p><p>At Ravinia, which just started selling tickets for<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/03/12/ravinia-2026-lineup-labrinth-lizzo-ricky-martin-music-chicago-summer-concerts" target="_blank" ><u> its upcoming season,</u></a> President and CEO Jeffrey P. Haydon estimates that about 5-10% of tickets sold come from speculative sites.</p><p>The practice became especially evident this season, Haydon said. As the Highland Park venue overhauls its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2025/02/13/ravinia-announces-a-75-million-renovation-plan-for-the-highland-park-venue" target="_blank" ><u>main pavilion</u></a>, its seat map is now different, but “the speculative ticketers have not figured that out yet, and were selling tickets to seats that do not exist.”</p><p>Haydon knows that no legislation will perfectly address the ever-shifting situation and smart online scammers, who are often operating outside of Illinois. However, he and other arts leaders are hopeful that it will act as a deterrent to begin to rein in the issue.</p><p>“I don't think any legislation ends up making anything perfect,” Haydon said. “But it starts to put those with maybe less altruistic motives on notice and hopefully to think twice.”</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: CTA inicia la construcción de la primera nueva estación de la extensión de la Línea Roja
entries.summary: <p>La Autoridad de Tránsito de Chicago (CTA) inició la construcción de la primera estación planificada de la extensión de la Línea Roja en un evento mediático estelar el viernes, que contó con la presencia del exjugador de los Chicago Bulls, Scottie Pippen.</p><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson y un grupo de funcionarios electos elogiaron el proyecto, afirmando que corrige una injusticia histórica hacia el extremo lado sur, que carece de transporte rápido.</p><p>Sin embargo, la amenaza de recorte de fondos federales por parte de la administración del presidente Donald Trump estaba sobre el inicio de la extensa extensión de $5.7 mil millones que va desde la 95th Street hasta la 130th Street. La ceremonia se llevó a cabo en la 115th Street y la Michigan Avenue, donde se construirá una de las cuatro nuevas estaciones a lo largo de esa ruta.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3a0000" name="module-3a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La CTA casi detuvo la construcción preparatoria el mes pasado, después de que el Departamento de Transporte (DOT) congelara $2 mil millones en subvenciones para el proyecto mientras alegaba examinar las prácticas de contratación de la CTA.</p><p>Ese dinero fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/03/24/judge-cta-funding-red-line-extension" target="_blank" >liberado hace un mes</a> después de que la CTA llevara a la administración a la corte y un juez federal en Chicago interviniera.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-99a5e0" name="image-99a5e0"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" proyecto de $5.7 mil millones que añadirá cuatro nuevas estaciones y llevará esta línea del metro desde su actual término en la 95th Street hasta un nuevo final en la 130th Street." a la construcción de la extensión de la Línea Roja, un proyecto de $5.7 mil millones que añadirá cuatro nuevas estaciones y llevará esta línea del metro desde su actual término en la 95th Street hasta un nuevo final en la 130th Street.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/Para el Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Sin embargo, esa orden judicial es temporal. El juez no tomará una decisión sobre si liberar permanentemente los fondos hasta junio. La CTA espera que el trabajo en el proyecto continúe hasta la apertura planificada en 2030.</p><p>Johnson evitó la pregunta cuando un periodista le preguntó si cree que se liberarán todos los fondos federales.</p><p>"Bueno, mira, no podemos predecir la locura que viene de la Casa Blanca. Lo que podemos predecir es nuestra capacidad para luchar y ganar", dijo Johnson.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a enfrentar a la administración de Trump para preservar la promesa de financiamiento federal para el proyecto de $5.7 mil millones.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>En sus comentarios preparados para la multitud, Johnson dijo que Trump está atacando a las ciudades demócratas con recortes de fondos y aludió a la congelación de fondos por parte de la administración debido a los requisitos de diversidad de la CTA al seleccionar contratistas.</p><p>El proyecto “demostrará lo que significa luchar contra la administración de Trump… porque de alguna manera él cree que invertir en personas de color es un acto criminal”, aseguró Johnson.</p><p>La extensión de la Línea Roja incluye cuatro estaciones planificadas cerca de las calles 103th, 111th, 115th y 130th.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" Una vez completado, facilitará el acceso a 25,000 empleos a aquellos que viven en el extremo lado sur, según la jefa interina de la CTA, Nora Leerhsen. La línea de tren debería reducir el tiempo de viaje al centro en media hora, afirmó.</p><p>Pippen asistió a la ceremonia de inauguración, pero no habló. Pippen es inversor en uno de los subcontratistas de la extensión, llamada “Structure Re-Right”, según un portavoz de la CTA.</p><p>Los funcionarios en Chicago han estado hablando sobre extender la class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los pasajeros esperan en la plataforma de la estación 95th Street el 26 de septiembre de 1969, el día que se inauguró la estación. entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Man admits opening fire near border agents during Operation Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>A man held for nearly six months on weapons charges admitted Monday that he fired a gun “in proximity of” U.S. Customs and Border Protection agents<b> </b>days before the deportation campaign known as Operation Midway Blitz <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/11/u-s-border-patrol-boss-greg-bovino-fellow-agents-expected-to-soon-leave-chicago" target="_blank" >began to wind down</a>.</p><p>Hector Gomez, 46, made the admission while pleading guilty to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/07/new-indictment-cites-shooting-near-border-patrol-agents-in-chicago-still-doesnt-charge-gunman" target="_blank" >illegally possessing a firearm</a> as a felon. Gomez also admitted that he is a Mexican citizen unlawfully present in the United States.</p><p>The claim that agents had been fired upon in early November was met with some skepticism, especially when no charges were announced against an alleged shooter. Even now, Gomez has admitted only to discharging his weapon “in proximity of”<b> </b>the agents, with no additional details.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/07/new-indictment-cites-shooting-near-border-patrol-agents-in-chicago-still-doesnt-charge-gunman" >New indictment cites shooting near Border Patrol agents in Chicago, still doesn’t charge gunman</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>There were no reports of anyone hit by gunfire<b> </b>in the Nov. 8 incident.</p><p>Still, federal prosecutors in Chicago now have their second guilty plea for a nonimmigration crime tied to Operation Midway Blitz. The feds <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >charged 32 known defendants</a> with such crimes. Twenty of them have been cleared, and four others are on track to have their cases dismissed.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/15/crime-chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-ice-border-patrol" target="_blank" >Anthony Gonzalez Alvarez</a> also pleaded guilty earlier this month to concealing information about a crime. In doing so, he admitted that he drove his Ford F-250<b> </b>into the rear of a vehicle driven by immigration officers.</p><p>Gomez entered his plea Monday before U.S. District Judge Matthew Kennelly, who scheduled Gomez’s sentencing for July 20. Gomez, who is in federal custody, spoke through a Spanish interpreter and told the judge he agreed with allegations read into the record by Assistant U.S. Attorney Jill<b> </b>Bhalakia.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" >Tracking every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s deportation blitz</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The charge carries a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison. Kennelly noted the prosecution would also likely lead to Gomez’s deportation.</p><p>Gomez’s arrest came after a chaotic day in Little Village<b> </b>near the end of the monthslong deportation campaign in the Chicago area.</p><p>The Department of Homeland Security initially reported on social media that “an unknown male driving a black Jeep fired shots at agents and fled the scene.”</p><p>In court Monday,<b> </b>Gomez admitted that, at 9:30 a.m. on Nov 8, he fired “at least two rounds” near CBP agents while driving a black Jeep Wrangler. Gomez later parked the vehicle, got out and pointed the gun toward an unidentified victim before getting back into the Wrangler.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Former Harold Ickes Homes complex in South Loop adding 80 apartments
entries.summary: <p>Construction started on the next phase of the redevelopment of a former South Loop public housing complex on Monday.</p><p>Developer The Community Builders was joined by project partners and city officials for the groundbreaking of Southbridge 1C, the third phase of Southbridge, a mixed-income development at the former Harold Ickes Homes site. </p><p>The new 12-story building, at 2305 S. State St. will include 80 apartments and 1,500 square feet of ground floor retail. Construction is expected to finish in June 2027.</p><p>The Community Builders completed Southbridge’s first two phases in 2021. </p><p>Funding has already been secured for the fourth phase, which will also offer 80 units, Will Woodley, senior vice president of real estate development for The Community Builders, said. </p><p>“We're excited to deliver homes and neighborhood amenities that help residents of all incomes live, work and thrive,” Woodley said.</p><p>Southbridge, a master-planned project designed by Gensler, is in a prime location. The South Loop project abuts the Cermak-McCormick Place Green Line stop and is near schools, parks and job centers, Woodley said. It’s also a connector to other neighborhoods, like Bronzeville and Chinatown.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Keith Pettigrew, CEO of the Chicago Housing Authority (left); Lissette Castañeda, commissioner of the Chicago Department of Housing; Mayor Brandon Johnson; Ald. Pat Dowell (3rd); Keith Pryor, deputy director of multifamily financing at the Illinois Housing Development Authority; and Will Woodley, senior vice president of real estate development for The Community Builders</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/Victor Hilitski for the Chicago Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“As the need for housing space changes, we recognize that the South Loop doesn't need to be an office monolith anymore,” Mayor Brandon Johnson said. “We can transform this into a lively and active neighborhood beyond the Monday through Friday 9-to-5 hours and make this a true live, work and play community — one that people don't just pass through but plant roots in.”</p><p>Ald. Pat Dowell (3rd) praised the project for its affordable units, retail and proximity to transit.</p><p>“Now that's a trifecta of the elements of what makes a viable urban community,” Dowell said. “But you throw on top of that proximity to open space, proximity to the Downtown, proximity to Chinatown, Bronzeville, McCormick Place, and Southbridge 1C is clearly a winning location for families and singles.”</p><p>The apartments will offer views of downtown, with amenities like in-unit laundry and quartz countertops. The building will also include a rooftop lounge, dog wash, resident storage and a gym.</p><p>Units include studios up to two-bedrooms. Of the 80 units, 44 will be affordable to households earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income and 29 of them will be for Chicago Housing Authority households with a right of return, according to the city.</p><p>When complete, Southbridge will have more than 800 mixed-income housing units and 65,000 square feet of commercial and community space. Rentals and for-sale homes are included in the development. Southbridge currently has just over 200 total units.</p><p>Phase three was supported by $11.9 million in tax increment financing that the City Council approved in July 2025. The CHA also contributed $10 million to the project.</p><p>Monday’s groundbreaking was also one of the first events attended by CHA CEO <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/17/chicago-housing-authority-appoints-keith-pettigrew-as-new-ceo" >Keith Pettigrew</a>, who last week started his four-year term at the nation's third-largest housing authority. </p><p>Pettigrew said he looks forward to working with Johnson, who had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/15/mayor-johnson-retired-ald-walter-burnett-cha-shut-down-hud" >pushing for retired Ald. Walter Burnett</a> to lead the CHA. </p><p>“Southbridge seems to be exactly the type of sustainable mixed-income, mixed-use community that will play a key part of CHA and Chicago’s future,” Pettigrew said. “So let's work hard together and do what we're here to do: To be creative, caring, committed in building and sustaining affordable housing for those in need in the city of Chicago.”</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Pritzker's accountability panel unveils 'reckoning' of feds' actions during Operation Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>It’s been months since President Donald Trump’s “Operation Midway Blitz” winded down in Chicago. But the wounds left behind remain fresh for the people who bore witness to the aggressive immigration campaign. </p><p>That pain was on display Monday during hours of emotional testimony recounting several violent interactions involving masked federal agents confronting and arresting residents, and deploying tear gas on neighborhood streets. </p><p>The mother of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/09/08/operation-midway-blitz-katie-abraham-illinois-dhs-kristi-noem-donald-trump" target="_blank" >Katie Abraham</a> was among the people who spoke Monday as a part of a two-day hearing held by the Illinois Accountability Commission. The state commission was created to investigate the actions of federal agents in Chicago last fall. </p><p>Abraham, a 20-year-old college student, was killed in Urbana following a hit-and-run drunk driving incident by an immigrant without legal status in January 2025. The Trump administration said Operation Midway Blitz was named in her honor.</p><p>But Abraham would have "wanted nothing to do with it," said her mom, Denise Lorence.</p><p>The Trump administration “used my daughter Katie's name in an attempt to give them cover for their disturbing tactics,” Lorence said. “Having my daughter's name used this way added a level of despair I didn't even know could exist.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0000" name="image-2c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7481988/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/218d5a8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/514dd2b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Denise Lorence speaks to the Illinois Accountability Commission during Monday's hearing at the Michael A. Bilandic Building in downtown Chicago. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/514dd2b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/344afaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/514dd2b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6499x3647+0+344/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2F1a%2Fef6c26a44eebb10b91f9538f48da%2Fiachearing-260428-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Denise Lorence speaks to the Illinois Accountability Commission during Monday’s hearing at the Michael A. Bilandic Building in downtown Chicago. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Illinois Accountability Commission <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/10/23/governor-jb-pritzker-commission-harassment-intimidation-abuse-federal-agents-operation-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >was formed by Gov. JB Pritzker</a> in October to hold federal immigration agents accountable for the aggressive tactics used during the deportation campaign and to collect and preserve evidence.</p><p>The commission — which doesn’t have subpoena or prosecutorial power — has spent months gathering testimony and reviewing body camera footage, bystander videos, law enforcement records, news reports and court filings, said the commission’s vice chair, Patricia Brown Holmes. </p><p>“We built a record of evidence that you, the public, can now judge for yourselves,” said Holmes, who called the commission's work a "reckoning."</p><p>“This is a durable record," she added, "and it exists because of the people of Illinois. People who grabbed their whistles and raised their cell phones, who brought lawsuits, produced investigative journalism and posted what they saw to their social media, who bore witness even when they didn't feel safe.”</p><p>The two-day hearings are unveiling that accounting. A second public hearing is scheduled for Tuesday at noon in the Michael A. Bilandic Building. The commission’s final report must be delivered to Pritzker by Thursday.</p><p>During Monday’s hearing, the commission played several videos from some of the major incidents that happened last fall and were widely reported on. That includes the raid of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/04/chicago-trump-immigration-deportation-ice-raid-south-shore-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >a South Shore apartment building</a> and confrontations between residents and federal agents in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/11/28/border-patrol-bodycam-video-tactics" target="_blank" >Little Village</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/24/immigration-agents-arrest-lake-view-protest-tear-gas" target="_blank" >Lake View</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/26/northwest-side-ice-arrests-tear-gas" target="_blank" >Old Irving Park</a> and Evanston.</p><p>Among the people who testified was Evanston resident Jennifer Moriarty. </p><p>She was walking to get her dry cleaning on Oct. 31 when federal agents "brake checked" a car, leading to a confrontation with the agents, the driver and residents on a busy Evanston street. </p><p> When Moriarty tried to film the aftermath of the crash, an agent grabbed her by the neck, threw her to the ground and cuffed her.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f40000" name="image-f40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium">
